transcript:
[doctor] hey sean nice to see you i heard you that you have some back pain
[patient] yeah it's nice to see you too it's been a while
[doctor] yeah
[patient] yeah yeah you know i had some back surgery and for some reason i always had lower
[doctor] left back pain when was your back surgery
[patient] it was in november of two thousand twenty
[doctor] was that long ago
[patient] yes
[doctor] wow what happened what did they do microdiscectomy my disk broke off and just wrapped around and connected to my spinal thecal sac sounds horrible it's not the poop sac though
[patient] that's good to know what kind of symptoms did you have with that horrible pain down my leg in my back just horrible awful things that you can think of
[doctor] horrible nerve pain it sounds like
[patient] i think so
[doctor] okay did they give you any meds for that they did i was highly highly dosed up on gabapentin
[patient] was that hard to withdraw from
[doctor] yeah it was terrible sounds like yeah there is certainly take too much of that and then stop suddenly you can definitely have some some issues okay so you have left side low back pain
[patient] mm-hmm
[doctor] does anything make it better or worse so my beautiful significant other always tells me to stretch and it always works
[patient] she sounds smart
[doctor] yeah she is so stretching helps okay what about any physical activities do they make it worse
[patient] a lot of bending over
[doctor] okay
[patient] and
[doctor] honestly it it just happens
[patient] you know randomly
[doctor] randomly okay so it hurt on the right side as well or just the left
[patient] depends on the day
[doctor] is it right around where you had surgery
[patient] generally
[doctor] okay did you try icing
[patient] no
[doctor] hmmm did you try any pain meds
[patient] yep
[doctor] what kind of pain meds did you
[patient] ibuprofen eight hundred milligrams a day
[doctor] it's quite a high dose okay let me do a quick physical exam on your back here if you bend forward does that hurt
[patient] not right now but normally it would
[doctor] okay so no pain with flexion how about if you stand back up
[patient] no
[doctor] okay no pain with extension either does it hurt while you're just standing here
[patient] yes
[doctor] okay so pain when you're weightbearing does it hurt when i push right here on the left side
[patient] yes
[doctor] pain with palpation to the left side around the l5 how about on the right side
[patient] no
[doctor] no pain with palpation to the right side okay i'm just gon na push down on your feet can you push back up on my hands
[patient] yes
[doctor] alright dorsiflexion is normal looks like that you have normal range of motion so that's good any pain down your legs
[patient] no
[doctor] great no nerve pain alright so we took an x-ray when you came in it looks like you do n't have any fractures or anything on the spinal bone that's good you can see where you had surgery it looks like everything has healed properly there so that's good news so what i think you did is you have a a muscle pull there from having a misalignment in your spine so what i'm gon na do is i'll prescribe some physical therapy for you you have great physical therapy here at the va so you can just come right to our physical therapist and that should help have you have you been there before
[patient] yes
[doctor] how do you feel about it
[patient] i really go to the dentist
[doctor] got it okay so i'm also gon na prescribe you some meloxicam instead of the ibuprofen that might help a little bit more with some of the pain management
[patient] is there any chance i can get some vicodin
[doctor] lem me check your record quick for for pain management it's kind of a strong drug for vicodin and we do n't really like to prescribe that unless it's totally necessary so why do n't we start with the meloxicam and i also write a referral to pain management and they can talk to you a little bit about some of your pain and maybe not using narcotics to manage it
[patient] how about some demerol
[doctor] hmmm sure i'll prescribe demerol
[patient] thank you
[doctor] yeah you're welcome any other problems going on today you want to talk about
[patient] no
[doctor] alright well it was nice to see you today
[patient] it's always nice to see you as well
[doctor] alright
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Low back pain, left side.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Sean Phillips is a pleasant 35-year-old male who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of left-sided low back pain.

The patient has been experiencing pain in the left side of his low back, generally localized around the surgical site of his microdiscectomy performed in 11/2020, but occasionally into the right side as well. Other than spontaneous flare-ups, his pain is worsened with excessive spinal flexion. Stretching and ibuprofen 800 mg daily are helpful, but he denies having tried icing the area.

SURGICAL HISTORY

The patient is status post lumbar microdiscectomy performed in 11/2020.

MEDICATIONS

The patient reports that, prior to his microdiscectomy in 2020, he was taking high-dose gabapentin for severe nerve pain through his back and lower extremities. He is currently taking ibuprofen 800 mg daily.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Musculoskeletal: Reports left-sided low back pain.

PHYSICAL EXAM

MSK: Examination of the lumbar spine: No pain with flexion or extension. Pain with weight-bearing. Pain with palpation on the left side around L5. No pain with palpation on the right side. Dorsiflexion is normal. Normal range of motion. No nerve pain in his lower extremities.

RESULTS

X-ray images of the lumbar spine were obtained and reviewed in office today. These reveal no evidence of fracture or other abnormalities in the spine or vertebrae. His previous surgical site is visible and appears to have healed properly.

ASSESSMENT

Low back pain, left side.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and radiographic findings today, I believe his symptoms are related to a muscle strain due to spinal misalignment. I recommend that we treat the patient conservatively with formal physical therapy and meloxicam. The patient requested a prescription for Vicodin and Demerol. I explained that Vicodin is a strong medication for this issue and that I would need to refer him to pain management for that, but I did provide him with a prescription for Demerol.
notes:
Subjective:
The patient, a male, presents with chronic lower back pain, specifically left-sided, which he describes as "horrible" and "awful." He reports that the pain is exacerbated by bending over, and sometimes he experiences pain on the right side as well. He mentions that stretching helps alleviate the pain. He has a history of back surgery in November 2020, which involved a microdiscectomy. He was prescribed gabapentin, which he had difficulty withdrawing from when he stopped taking it suddenly. He currently takes ibuprofen 800mg daily for pain management.

Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient had no pain with flexion or extension, but did experience pain with weight-bearing and palpation to the left side around the L5. Dorsiflexion was normal with a normal range of motion.
Test Results: X-ray results showed no fractures or abnormalities on the spinal bone.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has chronic left-sided lower back pain, likely due to a muscle pull from a misalignment in his spine.
Problem list: Chronic lower back pain; musculoskeletal strain of the lumbar region; history of back surgery.

Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam for pain management. I also referred the patient to pain management for further evaluation and to discuss alternative pain management options. I referred the patient to physical therapy to help with the muscle pull. I prescribed demerol.

Follow-up: The patient will follow up with the physical therapist and the pain management specialist.

Subjective:
The patient, Sean, is a male who presents with a history of back pain, specifically lower left back pain, which began after a microdiscectomy surgery in November 2020. He describes the pain as "horrible" and "awful," with symptoms including nerve pain down his leg. He was prescribed gabapentin in high doses for this condition, which he found difficult to withdraw from. He reports that stretching helps alleviate the pain, but bending over and physical activities make it worse. He also mentions that the pain is not always present, but can occur randomly.

Past medical history: The patient had a microdiscectomy surgery in November 2020 for a disk break that wrapped around and connected to his spinal thecal sac.

Review of systems: The patient reports pain in his lower back, but does not mention any other symptoms in other organs.

Current medications: The patient is currently taking gabapentin, and will be prescribed meloxicam and demerol.

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam:
The patient had no pain with flexion or extension, but reported pain when standing and when palpation was performed on the left side around the L5. Dorsiflexion was normal, and the patient had normal range of motion. There was no pain down his legs.

Test Results:
The patient had an x-ray, which showed no fractures or issues with the spinal bone, and the area where he had surgery had healed properly.

Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has a muscle pull due to a misalignment in his spine, confirmed by the physical exam and x-ray results. The patient has left side low back pain; confirmed.

Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam for the patient's pain management, and referred him to pain management for further evaluation and discussion of alternative pain management options. I also prescribed demerol. The patient will start physical therapy to address the muscle pull and misalignment in his spine. He will see a physical therapist at the VA.

Follow-up:
The patient will follow up with me in [NOT MENTIONED].

The patient describes the pain as "horrible" and "awful," and states that it has been accompanied by nerve pain. The patient reports being highly dosed on gabapentin for the pain and experiencing difficulty withdrawing from it. The patient also reports that stretching helps alleviate the pain, but bending over and weight-bearing activities exacerbate it.

Past medical history: The patient had back surgery in November 2020, a microdiscectomy, and was prescribed gabapentin for pain management.

Review of systems: The patient reports no symptoms in other organs or systems.

Current medications: I prescribed gabapentin for nerve pain, which the patient was taking at a high dose of 800mg per day.

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Test Results: The patient underwent an X-ray, which showed no fractures or abnormalities in the spinal bone, and showed that the area where the patient had surgery had healed properly.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a muscle pull in the left lower back due to a misalignment in the spine.

Problem list: Lower left back pain; muscle pull in left lower back; history of microdiscectomy.

Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam 7.5mg per day for pain management, and referred the patient to physical therapy. I also wrote a referral to pain management to discuss alternative pain management options. I prescribed demerol, but only after discussing the risks of narcotics with the patient.

Follow-up: The patient will return for follow-up in two weeks to assess the effectiveness of the physical therapy and pain management plan.

The patient reports nerve pain that was treated with high-dose gabapentin, which was difficult to withdraw from. The patient's partner recommends stretching, which provides relief. The patient also reports pain with bending over, and pain that occurs randomly. The pain can be exacerbated by physical activities such as bending over and is worse when weight-bearing. The patient has tried ibuprofen 800mg daily and reports that it is a high dose.

Past medical history: The patient had a microdiscectomy surgery in November 2020 due to a herniated disk that broke off and wrapped around the thecal sac.
